skill.name: pysam
skill.description: Genomic file toolkit for reading and writing SAM/BAM/CRAM alignment files, VCF/BCF variant files, FASTA/FASTQ sequences. Supports region extraction, coverage calculation, and NGS data processing pipelines.
skill.summary: Process SAM/BAM/CRAM, VCF/BCF, and FASTA/FASTQ genomic files using the pysam Python library.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 442 static analysis findings are false positives caused by domain-specific terminology confusion. 'SAM' in this skill refers to the Sequence Alignment/Map bioinformatics format, not Windows Security Account Manager. Backtick code blocks are standard markdown formatting for Python documentation examples, not Ruby shell execution. The fetch() calls are pysam library methods for genomic region queries, not HTTP fetch API calls. File operations are legitimate genomic data processing, the core purpose of this skill. No executable scripts or malicious patterns exist. The skill is safe to publish.

low_findings.title: Bioinformatics terminology confused with security threats
low_findings.description: The static analyzer confused bioinformatics domain terminology with security threats. 'SAM' refers to Sequence Alignment/Map format (not Windows Security Account Manager), 'fetch()' is a pysam library method for genomic region queries (not HTTP API), Python .keys() calls on variant records were flagged as certificate/key file access, and bioinformatics tool references were flagged as network scanning tools. All occurrences are in documentation markdown files with code examples for the legitimate pysam library.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confidently determined by reading source files and confirming all flagged terms are standard bioinformatics vocabulary used in their correct domain context.

low_findings.title: Markdown code block formatting flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: Over 300 findings flagged markdown code block backticks as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are standard markdown fenced code blocks containing Python code examples for the pysam library. No actual shell command execution occurs. The skill contains no executable scripts or shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All files are markdown documentation with standard fenced code blocks. No Ruby code or shell scripts exist in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Heuristic combination patterns are not malicious
low_findings.description: The heuristic analysis flagged 'Code execution + Network + Credential access' and 'Filesystem + Credentials + Network' as dangerous combinations. Review confirms: code execution refers to Python code examples in documentation, network refers to documentation URLs (pysam.readthedocs.io), and no actual credential access or exfiltration exists. The combination is characteristic of a legitimate bioinformatics tool that processes local genomic files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Read all source files and confirmed no credential access or data exfiltration exists. Heuristic combined benign patterns present in any bioinformatics skill documentation.

content.user_title: Process and Analyze Genomic Sequencing Data with Pysam
content.value_statement: Bioinformatics workflows require reading and analyzing complex genomic file formats like BAM, VCF, and FASTA. This skill provides ready-to-use documentation and code templates for processing sequencing alignment files, genetic variants, and reference sequences using the pysam Python library.
actual_capabilities: Read and write SAM/BAM/CRAM alignment files with region-based queries and coverage calculation
Analyze VCF/BCF variant files including genotype data, sample information, and quality filtering
Extract reference sequences from indexed FASTA files by genomic coordinates
Calculate per-base coverage statistics using pileup analysis
Process FASTQ sequencing reads with quality score access and filtering
Execute samtools and bcftools commands through the pysam Python interface
limitations: Does not include sample genomic data files; you must provide your own BAM, VCF, or FASTA files
Cannot execute pysam code directly; provides documentation and templates for AI-assisted code generation
Large genomic files may require significant memory and disk resources for processing
Index files (.bai, .fai, .tbi) are required for random access operations on genomic regions

use_cases.title: Calculate Coverage for Targeted Gene Panels
use_cases.description: A bioinformatician needs per-base coverage statistics across specific genomic regions to assess sequencing quality. This skill provides the pysam pileup workflow to calculate and report coverage for targeted regions.

use_cases.title: Extract and Filter Genetic Variants from VCF Files
use_cases.description: A clinical geneticist needs to filter and analyze genetic variants from whole-exome sequencing data. This skill provides workflows for reading VCF files, accessing variant records, and filtering by quality or allele frequency.

use_cases.title: Verify Variant Calls Using Alignment Reads
use_cases.description: A computational biologist needs to confirm whether variant calls are supported by the underlying sequencing reads. This skill provides the integrated workflow combining BAM alignment and VCF variant analysis.
use_cases.target_user: Computational biologist or genomics pipeline developer

prompt_templates.title: Read a BAM File and List Reference Sequences
prompt_templates.prompt: Open a BAM alignment file using pysam and print the list of reference sequence names and their lengths.
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic file inspection for new pysam users

prompt_templates.title: Calculate Coverage for a Specific Genomic Region
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pysam pileup to calculate per-base coverage for region chr1:10000-20000 and identify positions with coverage below 10x.
prompt_templates.scenario: Targeted coverage analysis for sequencing quality assessment

prompt_templates.title: Filter VCF Variants by Quality and Read Depth
prompt_templates.prompt: Open a VCF file with pysam, iterate through variants, and filter to keep only variants with QUAL greater than 30 and depth (DP) greater than 20. Write the filtered variants to a new VCF file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Variant quality filtering for downstream analysis

prompt_templates.title: Integrated Variant Verification with BAM and VCF
prompt_templates.prompt: For each high-quality variant in a VCF file, fetch the aligned reads from the corresponding BAM file at that position. Count how many reads support the reference allele versus the variant allele and report the allele balance.

output_examples.input: Calculate the average coverage for all genes in a BED file using a BAM alignment.
output_examples.output: The skill provides a complete pysam workflow: opening the BAM file, reading each region from the BED file, calculating coverage per base using pileup, and reporting the average coverage for each gene region in a structured summary table.

output_examples.input: Filter a multi-sample VCF to keep only high-quality SNP variants present in at least one sample.
output_examples.output: The workflow demonstrates iterating through VCF records with pysam, checking variant type (SNP), filtering by quality metrics, examining genotype data per sample, and writing the filtered results to a new VCF file.

output_examples.input: Extract the reference sequence for a gene region and compute its reverse complement.
output_examples.output: The skill provides the FastaFile workflow: indexing the reference genome FASTA, fetching the gene region by coordinates, computing the reverse complement, and returning both the forward and reverse complement sequences with their coordinates.

best_practices: Always create index files (.bai, .fai, .tbi) before performing random access operations on genomic files
Use 0-based coordinates when calling pysam methods directly and 1-based strings for samtools-style region queries
Close file handles explicitly with close() after completing file operations to release system resources
anti_patterns: Using repeated fetch() calls for per-base analysis instead of the more efficient pileup() method
Modifying query_qualities in-place after changing query_sequence without creating a copy first
Mixing 0-based and 1-based coordinate systems without explicit conversion notes in the code

faq.question: What genomic file formats does pysam support?
faq.answer: Pysam supports SAM, BAM, and CRAM alignment files, VCF and BCF variant files, and FASTA and FASTQ sequence files.

faq.question: Do I need index files to use pysam?
faq.answer: Random access to specific genomic regions requires index files. BAM files need .bai, FASTA files need .fai, and compressed VCF files need .tbi indexes.

faq.question: What coordinate system does pysam use?
faq.answer: Pysam uses 0-based left-closed right-open coordinates for method parameters. Region strings passed to fetch() use 1-based samtools convention.

faq.question: Can pysam run samtools and bcftools commands?
faq.answer: Yes, pysam provides Python wrappers for samtools and bcftools commands like sort, index, and view through pysam.samtools and pysam.bcftools modules.

faq.question: Does this skill include sample data files?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides documentation and code templates only. You need to provide your own genomic data files in BAM, VCF, FASTA, or FASTQ format.

faq.question: What is the difference between SAM and BAM format?
faq.answer: SAM is a plain text alignment format, while BAM is its compressed binary equivalent. BAM files are smaller and support random access via indexes.
